steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence reads: “New Mexico governor [blank] the first woman to serve in that role in the state’s history — took office on January 1, 2011.” The phrase “the first woman to serve in that role in the state’s history” is an appositive that renames the governor. It ends with a closing em dash before “took office.” An appositive set off by an em dash on one side must have a matching em dash on the other side.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Susana Martinez,’: uses a comma to open the appositive, but the appositive closes with an em dash — mismatched punctuation. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Susana Martinez:’: a colon introduces lists or explanations; it cannot open an em-dash appositive. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Susana Martinez’: no punctuation at all leaves the appositive without an opening marker, making it ungrammatical with the closing em dash. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘Susana Martinez—’: opens with an em dash to match the closing em dash after “history,” correctly setting off the appositive. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
